does: This paper introduces MetaAlign, a method enabling large language models to dynamically align with diverse human preferences at inference time rather than being locked into static alignment during training. The core contribution involves training LLMs on a specially constructed MetaAlign Dataset that equips models to interpret and respond to preference specifications provided in the input prompt itself. The approach allows a single model to adapt its outputs across different preference dimensions—whether explicit instructions or implicit contextual cues—without requiring retraining or parameter updates. Experimental validation demonstrates that models trained with this method effectively handle preference alignment across various scenarios at inference time, moving beyond the limitations of conventional one-size-fits-all alignment approaches.
connects: This work builds on the established alignment literature, particularly RLHF and Direct Preference Optimization, which have become standard techniques for making LLMs safer and more useful. However, MetaAlign directly addresses a fundamental limitation of these existing methods: they embed specific preference sets into model parameters during training, resulting in inflexible models that cannot adapt to varied user preferences without retraining. The paper responds to growing recognition in the field that human preferences are inherently diverse and context-dependent, making static alignment suboptimal for real-world deployment. It connects to broader work on prompt-based model control and in-context learning, extending these ideas to the alignment domain by treating preference specification as a form of inference-time conditioning rather than a training-time commitment.
enables: MetaAlign opens pathways toward more flexible and practical alignment systems where a single deployed model serves heterogeneous user bases with different values and requirements. This work enables future research on scaling preference alignment without multiplying model variants, reducing computational overhead for applications needing to respect diverse ethical frameworks or stylistic preferences simultaneously. The approach points toward dynamic alignment as a general principle for language models, potentially extending beyond preferences to other forms of controlled generation. It also suggests new directions for studying how models can interpret and generalize from preference specifications, including how to represent conflicting preferences and how to ensure robust alignment when users specify contradictory guidance at inference time.
